title: Audio Effect Rack — Parallel Processing
summary: Process audio through multiple effect chains simultaneously. Each chain has its own effects and a volume knob to blend back. Used for parallel compression, frequency-split effects, and complex FX macros.
steps: Add Audio Effect Rack (from Audio Effects in the browser) to any audio or instrument track
Click 'Add Chain' to add a parallel processing path
The default chain (labelled '1') processes the dry signal. Additional chains receive the same input
For parallel compression: Chain 1 = no effects (dry). Chain 2 = heavy Compressor (ratio 8:1, slow attack). Blend Chain 2 volume to taste — usually -6 to -3dB for the compressed chain
For frequency-split effects: Chain 1 with LP filter + reverb (lows into reverb). Chain 2 with HP filter + no reverb (highs stay dry)
Macro knobs map to multiple parameters across chains — Cmd+M to map
tips: Parallel compression is the most common use: the dry signal retains its dynamics and transients, while the heavily compressed version adds density and sustain. You blend the two to taste
Unlike series processing (effect after effect in a chain), parallel processing means both paths get the same input. The result is the sum of both outputs
Macro knobs are powerful here: map 'Crush Amount' to Drive on a Saturator in one chain, so you can automate how much grit is blended into the signal
The Dry/Wet controls in the rack itself (separate from individual device Dry/Wet knobs) let you bypass all chains at once with one knob
practice_hint: Try parallel compression on your DRUMS group: add Audio Effect Rack, create two chains. Chain 1 = no effects (dry). Chain 2 = Compressor at ratio 8:1, attack 1ms, release 50ms. Set Chain 2 volume to -6dB. Compare with/without Chain 2.
ableton_version_note: Available in all editions of Live 9+.
